title: Popular raw dog, cat food sold in Illinois, other states recalled for salmonella, listeria
summary: Recent reports have flagged recalls of dog and cat food, including popular raw formulas sold in Illinois and other states, because of Salmonella and Listeria contamination. The Food Poison Journal first reported a Salmonella-related recall on August 25, and NBC 5 Chicago followed on August 27 with additional coverage of a raw food recall involving both pathogens. Both dogs and cats are affected. Pets infected with Salmonella or Listeria may develop vomiting, diarrhea, fever, lethargy, and decreased appetite, and some animals can carry the bacteria without showing symptoms. The risk also extends to people in the household, especially children, older adults, and immunocompromised individuals, who can become ill from handling contaminated food or touching pets and surfaces. Pet owners should review recall details, stop feeding the affected product, and consult a veterinarian if symptoms appear.

title: Acetaminophen rises to top 10 pet poisons in 2025
summary: Pet Poison Helpline has released its 2025 Annual Report Card, reflecting a full year of veterinary toxicology consultations and poisoning case data across the United States. Alongside the report, multiple outlets published the organization’s 2025 Top 10 Pet Poisons list, with DVM360 noting that acetaminophen—a common over-the-counter pain reliever—moved into the top 10 for the first time. PR Newswire also circulated the ranking, underscoring its importance for pet owners. The findings highlight that everyday human medications and household items remain significant poisoning risks for companion animals. The report is especially valuable for pet owners monitoring potential hazards, reinforcing the need to keep medications secure, recognize symptoms of poisoning early, and seek expert guidance immediately. This is a useful resource for understanding annual poison trends and preventing accidental exposure.

title: Daffodils can be 'dangerously toxic' to dogs and cats pet owners warned
summary: A string of late-winter pet-safety incidents and veterinary warnings has highlighted toxic hazards facing dogs and cats. In Lincoln, Nebraska, police are investigating after a woman’s dog was poisoned; one report ties the poisoning to a burglary. Meanwhile, veterinarians and news outlets are warning owners about lesser-known toxins. Wales Online issued a 'do not delay' alert about a lesser-known toxin affecting dogs, Vet Times reminded readers that rhododendron and azalea are poisonous to dogs and cats, and the Derry Journal cautioned that daffodils can be dangerously toxic to both species. Collectively, the reports show that poisoning can stem from both deliberate harm and common household or garden plants, and that rapid veterinary intervention is critical. The developments matter because they underline the need for vigilance indoors and outdoors, and because many toxins can be fatal if treatment is delayed.

sources.name: KOLN | Nebraska Local News, Weather, Sports | Lincoln, NE

title: Xylitol, a commonly used compound in gum and medications, poses danger to pets
summary: News outlets are highlighting the risks of xylitol, a popular sugar substitute found in chewing gum and medications, to pets. Reports from MSN and CBS News on February 26, 2026, warn that even small amounts of xylitol can be dangerous to cats and other animals, with potential effects including low blood sugar, seizures, liver damage, and death. Because xylitol appears in many everyday products—including sugar-free gum, candies, baked goods, toothpaste, and some medicines—pet owners may not realize their animals can reach it. The coverage serves as a reminder to keep xylitol-containing items out of pets' reach, read labels carefully, and seek veterinary care or contact a poison control center immediately if ingestion is suspected. The alert matters because quick action can greatly improve outcomes for affected pets.

title: RCL Foods recalls pet food over Salmonella contamination risk
summary: Multiple pet food products have been recalled in early 2026 due to bacterial contamination, including Salmonella, Listeria, and E. coli. Reports from Food Poison Journal, PetfoodIndustry, and PIX11 describe recalls involving dog treats and other pet foods, with RCL Foods among the companies named. Salmonella is the most common pathogen cited, but one recall also involves Listeria and E. coli. Affected animals include dogs and potentially other pets. These pathogens can cause vomiting, diarrhea, fever, and lethargy in animals; some infections, such as Salmonella, can also spread to humans, posing a zoonotic risk. Pet owners should check recall notices, stop feeding affected products, store food safely, and contact a veterinarian if symptoms appear. Staying informed helps protect both pets and household members.

title: Wide range of dog and cat food in Japan found to contain toxic PFAS: research
summary: Recent reports underscore overlapping pet food safety risks in Japan, the United States, and Ireland. In Japan, research found toxic PFAS—so-called 'forever chemicals'—in a wide range of dog and cat food, raising concerns about long-term exposure and bioaccumulation. Separately, Mamavation published a purchasing guide for dog owners focused on natural, organic, and non-toxic options that minimize pesticide and chemical exposure. In Ireland, the Irish Mirror warned dog and cat owners that common Easter foods such as chocolate, raisins, and xylitol-sweetened treats can be toxic. Together, these stories highlight the need for pet owners to scrutinize ingredients, packaging, and seasonal hazards, and to choose safer products to reduce cumulative health risks for dogs and cats.

title: Hidden Killers in Your Home: Common Toxins Poisoning Pets Every Day - A-Z Animals
summary: Pet safety experts are warning that household toxins pose a daily poisoning risk to companion animals, with seasonal holidays amplifying the danger. A-Z Animals highlights common 'hidden killers' in homes that can poison pets every day, while the Pet Poison Helpline specifically cautions that Easter baskets and decorations can introduce serious hazards such as lilies, chocolate, and candy containing xylitol. Cats are especially vulnerable to lily toxicity, which can cause kidney failure, and dogs are at risk from chocolate and xylitol. These reports underscore that everyday items and holiday décor can become life-threatening when pets are curious or unattended. The guidance matters because prevention is the most effective way to avoid emergency vet visits and potential fatalities. Pet owners are urged to review their homes and holiday preparations for toxic substances, secure dangerous items, and know what to do if exposure is suspected.

title: These spring flowers may be toxic to cats and dogs — pet parents beware
summary: As spring arrives, multiple pet-safety reports are warning owners that common household items and seasonal plants can be dangerous to cats and dogs. A vet-reviewed Catster guide highlights 16 everyday foods toxic to cats, including onions, garlic, chocolate, and items containing xylitol. Meanwhile, spring flower warnings from Yahoo Lifestyle Canada, The Spruce Pets, and Daily Paws identify tulips, lilies, daffodils, azaleas, and hyacinths as potentially toxic, especially to cats. The bulbs and plant tissues can cause gastrointestinal upset, drooling, lethargy, and more serious organ damage depending on the plant and amount ingested. Pets affected include cats and, for some reports, dogs. The cluster underscores that routine spring activities—cooking, gardening, and bringing home bouquets—can expose pets to hidden toxins. Owners should identify plants, store risky foods securely, and seek veterinary care immediately if they suspect ingestion.

title: Raw dog food recall grows to more than 180 lots amid contamination concerns and reported illnesses
summary: During mid-to-late May 2026, several U.S. dog food recalls and broader pet toxin warnings highlighted serious safety risks for pet owners. Albright’s Raw Pet Food recalled select chicken recipe products for dogs over potential Salmonella contamination, prompting advisories across multiple states. Separately, Raaw Energy expanded a recall of raw dog food due to Listeria monocytogenes, with reported illnesses and more than 180 lots affected. AllProvide also recalled dog food sold in several states over possible plastic contamination. In addition, veterinary and consumer outlets warned that lilies, alstroemeria, and certain essential oils can be toxic to cats and dogs. The alerts primarily affect dogs, but cats are also at risk from common household plants and oils. These issues matter because contaminated food can cause gastrointestinal illness in pets and zoonotic infections in people, while everyday toxins can trigger severe or life-threatening reactions.

title: 30 Common Houseplants Toxic to Pets (A to Z): Cat, Dog, and Bird Safety Guide
summary: As spring and summer gardening season arrives, multiple outlets are warning pet owners that common ornamental plants can be toxic to cats, dogs, and birds. A USA Today report advises gardeners to avoid species harmful to cats, while Encyclopedia Britannica explains that lilies are especially dangerous to felines—ingestion of pollen, petals, or vase water can cause acute kidney failure. Farmers Almanac has also published an A-to-Z guide covering 30 common houseplants that threaten cats, dogs, and birds. The reports underscore that both outdoor garden beds and indoor plant collections can expose pets to toxins, potentially leading to severe illness or emergency veterinary care. Pet owners should identify toxic plants, remove or replace them with pet-safe alternatives, and contact a veterinarian or poison helpline immediately if a pet chews or ingests any suspect foliage.

title: Recalled Puppy Formula Neglecting Sickened Shelter Pups
summary: A reported pet-safety incident on June 4, 2026, involves a recalled puppy formula that is said to have neglected or failed to protect shelter puppies, leaving the dogs sickened. Covered by both Truth about Pet Food and Google News, the case focuses on young dogs—particularly vulnerable shelter puppies—who may have been harmed by a formula that was unsuitable, contaminated, or subject to a recall. Because puppies have specialized nutritional needs and weaker immune systems, unsafe formula can lead to serious illness, malnutrition, or worse. The incident underscores the importance of monitoring pet food recalls, verifying products before feeding them, and acting quickly if a puppy shows signs of illness. Caregivers, shelters, and pet owners should check recall notices, remove any affected formula from use, and consult a veterinarian promptly if symptoms appear.

title: Pet owners in 20 states need to know about this expanded food recall that poses serious neurological risks
summary: A nationwide recall of freeze-dried pet food has expanded to cover multiple dog and cat products sold under the Go Raw and Steve’s Real Food brands. The recall, which began in early June 2026, centers on potentially low thiamine—vitamin B1—levels in the food. Thiamine is essential for pets’ nervous system function, and a deficiency can cause serious neurological problems such as tremors, weakness, abnormal posture, and seizures. Reports from outlets including KVUE, the Houston Chronicle, MLive, and USA Today indicate the recall now affects consumers in about 20 states, with warnings from the FDA and state agencies. The recalled items are freeze-dried, raw-style diets marketed for both dogs and cats. Pet owners who have purchased these products should stop feeding them immediately and monitor their animals for signs of illness.

title: Pet Poison Helpline Named a Minnesota Star Tribune Top Workplace for the 16th Time
summary: Pet Poison Helpline, a 24/7 animal poison control service, has been named a Minnesota Star Tribune Top Workplace for the 16th time. The Star Tribune awards the designation based on confidential employee feedback about culture, leadership, and engagement. The honor highlights the organization’s ability to attract and retain experienced veterinary and toxicology staff, which directly affects the quality, speed, and consistency of the emergency guidance it provides to pet parents and veterinary professionals across the country. While this is not an active poisoning incident, it is relevant to pet safety because a stable, knowledgeable helpline can mean faster, more accurate advice when a pet ingests a potentially toxic substance. Affected animals are broadly “other”/pets in general. The recognition underscores the importance of keeping reliable, accessible poison-control resources available to the animal community.

title: Voluntary Recall of Two Lots of PEDIGREE® Can High Protein Chopped Chicken & Duck Flavor Wet Dog Food Due to Potential Fraudulent Distribution of Product Which May Contain Foreign Material
summary: Mars Petcare has voluntarily recalled two lots of PEDIGREE® High Protein Chopped Chicken & Duck Flavor canned wet dog food after the FDA announced the product may contain sharp metal and plastic foreign material. The recall, reported widely on July 2–4, 2026, is believed to be linked to potentially fraudulent distribution of the affected lots. The canned food was sold nationwide, so a broad range of dog owners may be impacted. No pet illnesses were confirmed in the provided coverage, but metal and plastic fragments can cause choking, dental damage, or internal injuries in dogs. The incident highlights how quickly pet food recalls can spread and why supply-chain vigilance matters. Industry observers also note that strong recall preparedness can help manufacturers identify and prevent contamination before products reach consumers, reinforcing the importance of both reactive and preventive safety measures in the pet food industry.
